our_argument_summary: Our review says the 2.26× ratio between longitudinal and latitudinal NMP drift components is extracted directly from published NOAA pole position data. It is straightforward data arithmetic, not a model prediction. Already referenced in WIN-007. Verdict: Std Model Explains.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt — PROS-002 (NMP acceleration, -> WIN-007) references NP.xy dataset
monitor/baseline/01-home.txt — line 48: 'NMP longitudinal dominance 2.26×' listed as prospective confirmed prediction
raw-text/03-predictions.txt — W019: 'NMP drift direction this week: Poleward dominates lateral (PENDING)' — related weekly test
monitor.py — not available in current raw-text directory; prior reviews (WIN-007) identified Domain #11 as the NMP monitoring domain
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No calculation pipeline identified for WIN-043. The dome's monitoring infrastructure (Domain #11 in monitor.py, per WIN-007 review) fetches NOAA NP.xy data and checks whether current longitude > 130°E. No code computes the 2.26× longitudinal/latitudinal ratio. No domain tests the ratio value, the drift direction decomposition, or the 'dominance' claim. The ratio appears only as a text claim on the dome's homepage, not as a computed output of any monitoring script.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither. The 2.26× ratio does not appear in any monitoring domain. Domain #11 (the closest related domain) validates only the pole's current longitude against a 130°E threshold. The ratio is a text-level assertion on the dome homepage with no automated validation whatsoever.
data_sources_used: NOAA NP.xy (pole position time series — same source as WIN-006, WIN-007, WIN-036)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The 2.26× ratio is stated without specifying the time window, velocity calculation method (instantaneous vs. average), or coordinate system (geographic vs. geomagnetic). This ambiguity allows the ratio to be recalculated over different intervals to maintain a match. If the ratio changes due to the pole's ongoing deceleration (documented since ~2019), the dome could simply update the value or adjust the time window.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Our review does not make specific code claims about WIN-043 individually. The grouped detail_evidence correctly states the ratio is 'straightforward data arithmetic from NOAA source.' No discrepancies identified between our claims and the dome's actual code infrastructure.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-043's specific claim (2.26× longitudinal/latitudinal ratio) has no monitoring domain. Prior curmudgeon review of WIN-007 identified Domain #11 as the NMP domain, but Domain #11 only checks current longitude > 130°E — it does not decompose drift into longitudinal/latitudinal components or compute their ratio. No other domain in the 39+ mapped domains addresses velocity component ratios. The 2.26× value appears only as homepage text, not as a monitored metric.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The NMP's trajectory is fully described by standard core-mantle boundary flux dynamics. Livermore et al. (2020, Nature Geoscience) explain the post-1990 acceleration as competition between Canadian and Siberian flux lobes, which inherently determines both the direction (predominantly longitudinal toward Siberia) and speed of pole motion. The 2.26× ratio is a mathematical consequence of the pole's observed trajectory — it requires no model at all, just NOAA data and a calculator. The dome renames the cause ('aetheric vortex threshold crossing') without changing the predicted value or providing an alternative derivation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: NOAA has published NP.xy pole position data continuously since at least the 1590s (historical reconstructions) with modern high-cadence tracking since the 1900s. The 2.26× ratio can be computed from any subset of this publicly available dataset. The dome model was created well after these measurements were published. The ratio was not predicted in advance of measurement — it was extracted from already-published NOAA coordinates and labeled a 'confirmed prediction.' The dome's own W019 weekly test ('NMP drift direction this week: Poleward dominates lateral') was registered 2026-03-12 and remained PENDING as of the last snapshot, suggesting even the dome's own tracking hasn't formally confirmed this as a prospective prediction with pre-registered numerical precision.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site derives the 2.26× ratio from dome geometry. The dome's core parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km) and field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) are axially symmetric — they depend only on radial distance from the disc center and have no mechanism for predicting the ratio of longitudinal to latitudinal drift components. To derive such a ratio, the model would need: (1) a mechanism for pole motion direction (which requires breaking axial symmetry), and (2) a way to decompose that motion into lat/lon components from dome geometry. Neither exists. The 2.26× value is adopted from NOAA observations, not derived from any dome equation.

holes_found.description: Our review claims WIN-043 is a 'duplicate of WIN-007' but the relationship is imprecise. WIN-007 claims post-1990 exponential acceleration of the NMP. WIN-043 claims a specific 2.26× longitudinal/latitudinal ratio. These are related (both use NOAA NP.xy data) but not strictly duplicates — WIN-043 makes a quantitative claim about drift direction decomposition that WIN-007 does not address. A dome defender could argue: 'WIN-007 is about the pole's speed; WIN-043 is about its direction. These are independent observables.' Our 'duplicate' framing is slightly inaccurate and could be challenged.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'duplicate of WIN-007' to 'reuses WIN-007's NOAA dataset without adding new predictive content' or 'derivative of the same NOAA trajectory data used in WIN-007.' The argument should be that the ratio is not independently predicted, not that it's literally the same claim. Alternatively, note: 'The 2.26× ratio is a mathematical consequence of the same NMP trajectory documented in WIN-007. Decomposing a single trajectory into directional components does not create an independent prediction — it is redundant arithmetic on the same dataset.'

holes_found.description: We do not verify the 2.26× value itself. Our review states the ratio is 'arithmetic from NOAA trajectory data' but never checks whether 2.26× is actually correct. If the dome's arithmetic is wrong, that would be a stronger argument. If the arithmetic is correct, we should acknowledge that and explain why correct arithmetic doesn't equal a prediction. Either way, we should have checked.
holes_found.recommendation: Compute the ratio from NOAA NP.xy data. The pole moved from approximately (82.7°N, 114.4°W) in 1990 to approximately (86.5°N, 139.3°E) in 2025. Longitudinal change: 114.4 + 139.3 = 253.7° (or ~106.3° accounting for shortest path). Latitudinal change: 86.5 - 82.7 = 3.8°. But this naïve calculation gives a much larger ratio than 2.26×, suggesting the dome may be computing instantaneous velocity components over a specific interval, or using a different coordinate decomposition. The ambiguity in how 2.26× is computed should be noted — if we can't reproduce the exact value from public data, the dome should specify its methodology.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence for WIN-043 is thin — only three sentences. Compared to most other WINs, this receives the least individual analysis. While it's part of a grouped block (WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063), the grouped block's examples cite WIN-042 and WIN-043 without giving WIN-043 its own substantive rebuttal. A dome defender could say we're dismissing the claim without engaging with the actual 2.26× ratio.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence or detail_extra: 'The 2.26× ratio describes the NMP trajectory's directional decomposition — how much faster the pole drifts in longitude vs latitude. This is straightforward trigonometry from the NOAA NP.xy coordinate series, not a model prediction. Any researcher with access to the same NOAA data arrives at the same ratio. The dome's axially symmetric geometry (H(r) depends only on radial distance) cannot predict longitude-specific pole motion, making the directional ratio an observation adopted post-hoc rather than derived from dome equations.'

holes_found.description: The verdict is 'Std Model Explains' but could arguably be 'Misleading' given that: (1) it's redundant with WIN-007 (same dataset), (2) the ratio is not derived from dome geometry, and (3) it inflates the win count by decomposing one observation into multiple 'predictions.' The grouped block's other WINs use both 'Std Model Explains' and 'Misleading' verdicts, and WIN-043 shares characteristics of both.
holes_found.recommendation: No change strictly necessary — 'Std Model Explains' is defensible since standard core dynamics explain the pole trajectory. But if consistency within the group matters, note that WIN-042 (same group) is 'Misleading' because it's a duplicate of WIN-037, while WIN-043 is 'Std Model Explains' despite being similarly redundant with WIN-007. The distinction is that WIN-042 uses the identical threshold and metric as WIN-037, while WIN-043 extracts a different metric (ratio) from the same dataset. This is a reasonable distinction but should be articulated.

stronger_arguments.description: The axial symmetry argument is devastating and underutilized. The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) is radially symmetric — it predicts the same field at all azimuths for a given radius. But the NMP's 2.26× longitudinal dominance is inherently an azimuthal claim: the pole moves faster in one angular direction than another. An axially symmetric model CANNOT predict directional preferences in pole motion. For the dome to predict the 2.26× ratio, it would need to break its own radial symmetry — which it has no mechanism to do. This means the ratio was necessarily adopted from observations, not derived from geometry. This argument applies equally to WIN-040 (SAA cell longitude) and should be cross-referenced.
sources: Livermore et al. 2020, Nature Geoscience — NMP trajectory driven by competition between geographically specific flux lobes (inherently azimuthally non-uniform)
Dome site H(r) formula — radially symmetric, no azimuthal dependence

stronger_arguments.description: The 2.26× ratio is epoch-dependent and will change over time, exposing the 'prediction' as a snapshot, not a stable model output. The NMP has been decelerating since approximately 2019 (from peak ~55 km/yr to ~40 km/yr by 2025). As the pole decelerates and potentially shifts direction, the longitudinal/latitudinal ratio will change. A genuine model prediction would specify the ratio as a function of time (or at least explain why it should be constant). The dome's static 2.26× value is a snapshot of one epoch's arithmetic, not a derived constant. When the ratio inevitably changes, the 'prediction' will fail silently — the dome will either ignore the change or update the value.
sources: NOAA WMM2020/2025 — NMP deceleration documented since ~2019
Chulliat et al. 2020, Earth, Planets and Space 72:27 — NMP speed variability

kernel_of_truth.description: The NMP does indeed exhibit longitudinal dominance in its recent drift — it has moved far more in longitude (crossing from the Western to Eastern Hemisphere) than in latitude (moving only a few degrees closer to the geographic pole). This asymmetric drift is a genuine geophysical observation. The dome model correctly identifies that the pole's motion is directionally biased, which is a real and somewhat underappreciated feature of recent geomagnetic change. Quantifying this as a ratio (2.26×) shows genuine engagement with the NOAA data.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three fatal problems: (1) The 2.26× ratio is computed from NOAA data that existed before the dome model claimed it. This is retrodiction, not prediction. (2) The dome's axially symmetric geometry cannot derive directional preferences — the ratio must be adopted from observations, not predicted from dome equations. (3) Standard geophysics fully explains the directional asymmetry: Livermore et al. (2020) show that the competition between Canadian and Siberian flux lobes on the core-mantle boundary drives the pole predominantly eastward (longitudinally), with the latitudinal component being a secondary effect of the Siberian lobe's geographic position. The dome adds no explanatory or predictive power.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-043 claims the North Magnetic Pole drift exhibits a 2.26× ratio between longitudinal and latitudinal velocity components. This appears as a 'Prospective Confirmed Prediction' on the dome's V51.0 homepage ('NMP longitudinal dominance 2.26×') and is listed in the grouped WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 cluster.

**What the Claim Actually Is**

The NMP has moved dramatically since 1990, crossing from the Canadian Arctic into the Eastern Hemisphere. The trajectory is predominantly eastward (longitudinal) with a smaller poleward (latitudinal) component. The 2.26× ratio quantifies this directional asymmetry: for every degree of latitude gained, the pole moves 2.26 degrees in longitude. This is observable directly from NOAA's NP.xy dataset.

**The Axial Symmetry Problem**

This is the most damaging argument against WIN-043 and it's one we underutilize. The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on radial distance r. It is axially symmetric — the same at every azimuth. An axially symmetric model predicts isotropic pole motion (equal probability in all directions) or radial-only motion. It CANNOT predict that longitudinal drift should dominate latitudinal drift by any specific ratio. The 2.26× value requires azimuthally non-uniform dynamics, which the dome model lacks.

In standard geophysics, the directional asymmetry arises from the specific geographic locations of competing flux lobes on the core-mantle boundary. The Canadian negative flux lobe (elongating 1970-1999) and the Siberian positive flux lobe create a pull that is primarily longitudinal because of their east-west positioning. This is inherently 3D, azimuthally complex, and non-derivable from any radially symmetric model.

**Epoch-Dependence**

The 2.26× ratio is not a physical constant — it depends on the time window over which drift is measured. The NMP's speed and direction have varied considerably: pre-1970 (~10 km/yr, predominantly latitudinal), 1990-2005 (~40-55 km/yr, predominantly longitudinal), 2019-present (~40 km/yr, decelerating). The ratio computed over 1990-2020 will differ from 2000-2025 or 2010-2025. A genuine model prediction would derive the ratio as a function of time from physical parameters. The dome's static 2.26× is a single-epoch snapshot that will become obsolete as the pole continues to decelerate and potentially shift direction.

**Verification Gap**

Our review states the ratio is 'arithmetic from NOAA trajectory data' without actually verifying the 2.26× value. A rough check: the pole moved from approximately (82.7°N, 245.6°E) in 1990 to (86.5°N, 139.3°E) in 2025. The longitudinal change is large (~106° via the shorter great-circle path), while the latitudinal change is small (~3.8°). The naïve ratio would be far larger than 2.26×. This suggests the dome is computing velocity components over a specific shorter interval, or using instantaneous rates at a particular epoch, or defining 'longitudinal' and 'latitudinal' in a non-obvious coordinate system. The dome does not specify its methodology, making the 2.26× value unverifiable without knowing the computation window and method. We should note this ambiguity.

**Relationship to WIN-007**

Our review calls WIN-043 a 'duplicate of WIN-007,' but this is imprecise. WIN-007 claims a post-1990 'phase transition' in NMP speed. WIN-043 claims a specific ratio in NMP direction. They use the same dataset (NOAA NP.xy) and describe the same physical phenomenon (recent NMP trajectory) from different angles. The correct framing is: WIN-043 extracts a second metric from the same trajectory data that WIN-007 already exploits, inflating the win count without adding predictive content. It's redundant arithmetic, not a literal duplicate.

**Verdict Assessment**

'Std Model Explains' is correct and defensible. The NMP trajectory and its directional decomposition are fully explained by core-mantle boundary flux dynamics (Livermore 2020, Aubert 2013). The dome provides no alternative derivation. The verdict could also be 'Misleading' (redundant with WIN-007, ratio not derived from dome), but 'Std Model Explains' captures the core issue: standard physics explains the observation completely.

**Recommendations**

1. Replace 'duplicate of WIN-007' with 'redundant arithmetic on the same NOAA dataset used in WIN-007'
2. Add the axial symmetry argument: the dome's radially symmetric field equation cannot predict directional preferences in pole motion
3. Note the epoch-dependence: the ratio will change as the pole decelerates, exposing it as a snapshot, not a prediction
4. Note the verification ambiguity: the dome doesn't specify how 2.26× is computed (time window, coordinate system, velocity method)

citations_verified: https://www.noaa.gov/ — NOAA main page, linked in detail_evidence. URL correct and active.
citations_unchecked: Livermore et al. 2020, Nature Geoscience — Referenced in WIN-007 review and this review's stronger arguments. Already verified in WIN-007 curmudgeon review (DOI resolves correctly). Claim about flux lobe competition driving predominantly longitudinal NMP drift is consistent with the paper's findings.
Chulliat et al. 2020, Earth, Planets and Space 72:27 — Referenced in stronger arguments for NMP speed variability and deceleration. Not verified via DOI resolution in this session.

NOAA NP.xy dataset — Referenced as the source for the 2.26× ratio. Dataset exists and is publicly accessible at https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/geomag/data/poles/NP.xy. Verified in WIN-007 curmudgeon review.
